title: Detection Equipment and Exposure Device Characteristics Review
narrationScript: You cannot feel any of this. Every fact you will ever know about a radiation field comes from an instrument, which makes instrument selection a life skill, and at Level Two the selection is your call. So this review starts with a habit. Before you reach for a meter, name the question. What is the rate right here, right now. That is a survey meter question. How much have I accumulated. That is a dosimeter question. Is something about to go wrong at my chest height. That is the alarming ratemeter. No instrument answers a question it was not built for. Then match the range to the task. A scintillation instrument is enormously sensitive. It reads fields down around a few thousandths of a millirem per hour, which makes it the tool for proving an area clean or for locating a weak or shielded source, and exactly the wrong tool inside a strong field, where it overloads. A Geiger Mueller instrument is quick, rugged, and fine for low rate checks, but drive it into a very intense field and the tube can saturate. Pulses pile up faster than the circuit can count them, and some instruments fold back and display nearly zero exactly where the field is highest. That failure mode has put radiographers in hospitals. Many uncompensated tubes also over-respond to low energy scatter, and scatter is most of what surrounds a collimated shot. The ion chamber is the radiographer's workhorse because it stays linear from a couple of millirem per hour up through the tens of roentgen per hour an open source can produce. That is why boundary surveys and every approach after every exposure belong to the ion chamber. Respect its manners, though. It responds slowly, so walk it in deliberately and let the reading settle. Never outwalk your meter. Selection also means checking the instrument's paperwork and its health. Typical rules require calibration about every six months and after any repair, with accuracy commonly within about twenty percent. Every working day starts with a battery check, a look for physical damage, and a response check against a check source. An instrument that fails any of those stays behind, period. The worn instruments get the same discipline. Radiographic operations typically require three at once. The direct reading pocket dosimeter, usually ranged zero to two hundred milliroentgen, zeroed before work, then read and recorded every day. The processed badge, which becomes your legal dose of record once the laboratory reads it. And the alarming ratemeter, typically set to sound near five hundred millirem per hour and calibrated at least yearly. Three devices, three time scales. The ratemeter guards this second, the pocket dosimeter scores the day, the badge certifies the record. If a pocket dosimeter ever reads off scale, radiographic work stops and the badge goes in for priority processing, because your true dose is unknown until it comes back. Now the machine in the middle of every gamma job, the exposure device. It is a portable shield, depleted uranium or tungsten, with an S shaped path through it so no straight line connects the stored capsule to the outside world. Its operating characteristics set your handling rules. A loaded device is allowed to read up to about two hundred millirem per hour right at the surface, which is why you handle it briefly, carry it by the handle, and never sit on it or store it where people linger. Do the arithmetic. Three minutes hugged against your body can cost about ten millirem. The retrieval train deserves a daily inspection, every piece. Drive cable, free of kinks and broken strands. Connector, within wear limits, latched positively, and never mixed between manufacturers, because a mismatched connector can hold during crank-out and let go when it matters. Guide tube, no crushed sections, laid out in wide gentle bends, because a tight bend raises crank effort and can hang the source partway home. Collimator supported so its weight never drags the tube. And learn your crank's normal feel and its normal number of turns. If resistance changes suddenly during retrieval, stop. Do not force it. Hold position, watch your survey meter, and treat the job as a source out condition until the meter proves otherwise. If the source is not home, back away and execute the emergency procedure, distance first, then notify the radiation safety officer. X-ray machines bring different characteristics. Warm the tube up after idle time, respect the duty cycle, verify the interlocks and warning lights, and control the key. Their gift is that off means off. A de-energized tube emits nothing. A gamma source is never off, and that is why the survey meter, on and in your hand, proves every retrieval, after every exposure, with no exceptions, for the rest of your career.
imagePrompt: A radiography crew chief compares readings between an ion chamber survey meter and a small scintillation detector beside an open equipment case while a gamma projector with drive cable, guide tube, and collimator sits staged on a steel fabrication floor.

sections.heading: Start With the Question, Then Pick the Instrument
sections.body:
No human sense detects ionizing radiation, so every decision you make is only as good as the instrument behind it - and at Level II, **selecting** that instrument is your call. Selection starts by naming the question:

- *What is the rate here, right now?* - a **survey meter** question, answered in mR/h.
- *How much have I accumulated?* - a **dosimeter** question, answered in mR.
- *Has the field at my body just become dangerous?* - the **alarming ratemeter's** question.

No instrument answers a question it was not built for: a dosimeter cannot warn you off your next step, and a ratemeter alarm is a warning device, not a measurement.

Then match the **operating range** to the task. The working span of radiography runs from the few-µR/h background of a cleared area, through the **2 mR/h** boundary line, past the **500 mR/h** ratemeter set point, up to the tens of R/h an open source produces at close range - and no single detector covers all of it honestly. Range, energy response, response time, ruggedness, and calibration status are the selection criteria; the figure below maps the three survey-instrument families across that span.

sections.caption: Typical operating ranges of scintillation, GM, and ion-chamber survey instruments against the dose rates a radiography job actually produces.

sections.heading: Ranges, Response, and Failure Modes
sections.body:
- **Ion chamber** - the radiography workhorse. It collects the charge made in an air volume and stays accurate and **linear from a couple of mR/h up through tens of R/h**, which is why boundary surveys and the approach after **every** exposure belong to it. Its manners: it responds slowly, so zero it, let readings settle, and never walk faster than the meter can answer.
- **Geiger-Müller (GM)** - sensitive, fast, and rugged; fine for low-rate checks and finding contamination-level fields. Its vice is **saturation**: in an intense field, pulses arrive faster than the circuit can count, and some instruments **fold back toward zero exactly where the field is highest**. A quiet GM in a place that should be hot is a red flag, not a relief. Many tubes also **over-respond to low-energy scatter** unless energy-compensated - and scatter is most of what surrounds a collimated shot.
- **Scintillator** - a crystal that flashes light per interaction, counted by a photomultiplier. Enormously sensitive: the tool for proving an area clean or localizing a weak, shielded source, and the wrong tool in strong fields, where it overloads.

All three live under the same discipline: **calibration about every 6 months** and after repair, with accuracy commonly within about **±20%** (typical 10 CFR 34-style requirements), plus a daily **battery check, physical inspection, and check-source response** before the first exposure. An instrument that fails any of these stays behind.

sections.heading: Running the Worn-Instrument Program
sections.body:
Radiographic operations typically require **three devices worn together**, and at Level II you run the program, not just wear the hardware.

- **Direct-reading pocket dosimeter** (typically 0–200 mR): zeroed before work, **read and recorded daily** - by everyone, including you. **Off-scale?** Radiographic operations cease, the event is reported, and the badge goes for **priority processing**; the true dose is unknown until it returns. Typical rules also require an annual response check, accurate within about ±20%.
- **Processed badge** (film, TLD, or OSL): the **legal dose of record**, laboratory-read, exchanged monthly or quarterly. Accurate, but blind in real time.
- **Alarming ratemeter**: worn on the torso, typically **set to alarm near 500 mR/h** and calibrated at least annually. It exists to catch the mistake you did not know you made - the source that never seated, the step inside a rope.

Three time scales: the ratemeter guards *this second*, the pocket dosimeter scores *today*, the badge certifies *the record*. Your Level II additions: review the crew's daily readings against the job's dose budget, investigate anomalies the same day, and keep the records that prove you did.

sections.title: Believe the hazard, not a quiet meter
sections.body: When an instrument's story contradicts the physics - a quiet GM where a source should be blazing, an alarm chirping while another meter looks calm - **believe the hazard, not the comfortable reading**. Back out, re-establish the field with a different instrument family (ion chamber first), and treat the area as hot until proven otherwise. Saturation, dead batteries, and energy response have all written accident reports.

sections.heading: Exposure Device Characteristics That Affect Safe Handling
sections.body:
A gamma projector is a portable shield - **depleted uranium or tungsten** around an **S-tube**, so no straight-line path connects the stored capsule to the world. Its operating characteristics set your handling rules:

- **Surface dose rates.** Device standards allow roughly **200 mrem/h at the surface, 50 mrem/h at 5 cm, and 2 mrem/h at 1 m** from a loaded device. Run the arithmetic: three minutes carried against the body at the surface rate costs about **10 mR**. Handling is brief and purposeful - carry by the handle, never sit on the device, never store it where people linger.
- **Mass.** Shielding is dense; a typical Ir-192 projector runs around **50 lb**. Plan lifts and tie-down points - a dropped or shifting device is a crush hazard and a shielding-integrity question at once.
- **The lock.** Engaged with the **key out** at all times except actual cranking; most designs latch the pigtail automatically on full retraction. Verifying the lock action is part of the daily check.
- **Connector compatibility.** Drive cables, pigtails, and guide tubes are **not interchangeable between manufacturers or models** - a mismatched connector can hold during crank-out and let go when it matters.
- **Never 'off.'** A gamma source emits continuously, which drives storage, security, transport plugs and port covers - and the survey discipline that ends every exposure.

sections.caption: The retrieval train from crank to collimator, with the six daily inspection points where retrieval failures start.

sections.heading: Crank Feel, Counts, and the X-ray Contrast
sections.body:
Operating characteristics show up in your hands. Learn the **normal crank effort and turn count** for your device and layout - then a change means something. If resistance rises suddenly during retrieval: **stop**. Forcing a kinked cable or a source hung in a tight guide-tube bend can detach the assembly and convert a problem into an emergency. Hold position, watch the survey meter, and treat the job as a **source-out condition** until the meter proves otherwise; if the source is not shielded, back away - distance first - secure the area, and notify the RSO. The position indicator and the crank's feel are aids; **only the survey meter is proof**.

X-ray machines bring a different character: **key control and interlocks** that must be verified, warning lights, a **warm-up schedule** after idle periods to protect the tube, and a **duty cycle** limiting continuous run time. Their structural gift is that a de-energized tube emits **nothing** - there is no capsule that has to come home. A gamma device never grants that comfort, which is why the approach survey after every exposure is the habit that defines this trade.

figures.title: The retrieval train: daily inspection points

checkQuestions.question: To set and verify the 2 mR/h restricted-area boundary, the most appropriate instrument is:
options: A calibrated ion-chamber survey meter with its daily checks complete
An alarming ratemeter held at arm's length
A direct-reading pocket dosimeter read at the rope line
An uncompensated GM pancake probe
checkQuestions.explanation: Boundary setting needs an accurate, linear rate measurement at low rates from a calibrated, daily-checked survey instrument. Ratemeters warn and dosimeters integrate - neither measures rate accurately - and uncompensated GM tubes over-respond to the low-energy scatter that dominates at the rope.

checkQuestions.question: A loaded projector reads 200 mR/h at its surface. Carrying it against your body for 3 minutes costs about:
options: 1 mR
10 mR
30 mR
100 mR
checkQuestions.explanation: Dose = rate × time = 200 mR/h × (3/60) h = 10 mR - the reason handling is brief, by the handle, and the device is never a seat or a workbench.

checkQuestions.question: Halfway through retrieval the crank effort spikes well above its normal feel. The correct response is to:
options: Crank harder to pull the source through the tight spot
Reverse direction rapidly several times to shake the cable free
Stop cranking, hold position, check the survey meter, and treat it as a source-out condition until the meter proves otherwise
Disconnect the drive cable and inspect the connector by hand
checkQuestions.explanation: Forcing or whipping a bound cable can detach the source assembly and turn a snag into an emergency. The meter - not hope - determines what happens next; a stranded source means the emergency procedure and the RSO.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about the alarming ratemeter is correct?
options: If it stays quiet, the approach survey can be skipped
It is typically set to alarm near 500 mR/h, calibrated at least annually, and serves as a warning device - not a measuring instrument
It records the wearer's legal dose of record
It must agree with an ion chamber within 5 percent
checkQuestions.explanation: The ratemeter is a worn warning device with a typical 500 mR/h set point and annual calibration; it backs up - never replaces - the survey meter and the processed badge.

checkQuestions.question: Which field condition most directly risks a source failing to return to its shielded position?
options: A kinked drive cable, a worn or mismatched connector, or a sharply bent guide tube
A survey meter overdue for its check-source response
Film loaded without lead screens
An exposure calculated at the wrong source-to-film distance
checkQuestions.explanation: Retrieval is mechanical: cable, connector, and tube condition decide whether the source comes home, which is why they are inspected every day. Instrument and technique errors matter, but they do not strand sources.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §5.4–5.5
Typical regulations: 10 CFR 34 (survey instruments, personnel monitoring, exposure devices)
Device standards such as ISO 3999 / ANSI N432 (dose-rate limits around exposure devices, general)
General radiation detection references: ion chamber, Geiger-Müller, and scintillation detector principles
